command_log: agent-browser open https://httpbin.org/status/404 && agent-browser get title && agent-browser snapshot
agent-browser open https://httpbin.org/html && agent-browser get title && agent-browser snapshot

grade.details: The agent successfully completed both steps. It navigated to https://httpbin.org/status/404 and correctly reported the 404 error (net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE). It then navigated to https://httpbin.org/html and correctly reported the page content as a Moby-Dick excerpt about the blacksmith Perth. Both steps were completed with accurate information.

agent_output:
Here's what I found:

## https://httpbin.org/status/404

**Error:** Navigation failed: `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE`

The browser refused to load the page because the server returned an HTTP 404 (Not Found) status code. The browser treats 4xx responses as navigation failures.

---

## https://httpbin.org/html

Browsing works normally. The page loaded successfully and contains a classic literary excerpt:

**Title:** (none set)  
**Content:** A passage from *Herman Melville's Moby-Dick* — specifically a section about the old blacksmith Perth, describing his tragic backstory and his work forging weapons on the ship's deck.

**1. https://httpbin.org/status/404 — Error encountered:**
The browser returned a navigation failure: `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE`. The server responded with an HTTP 404 Not Found status code, which Chromium treats as a hard navigation error and refuses to load the page.

**2. https://httpbin.org/html — Browsing works normally:**
The page loaded successfully and contains an excerpt from **Herman Melville's *Moby-Dick***, specifically a passage about the old blacksmith Perth. The page title is "Herman Melville - Moby-Dick" and includes a long paragraph describing Perth toiling at his portable forge on the ship's deck, followed by the sad backstory of how he lost his home and family to alcoholism.

| Population | Year Reached |
|-----------|-------------|
| 1 billion | 1804 |
| 2 billion | 1927 |
| 3 billion | 1960 |
| 4 billion | 1974 |
| 5 billion | 1987 |
| 6 billion | 1999 |
| 7 billion | 2011 |
| 8 billion | 2022 |

Notable pattern: it took 123 years to go from 1 to 2 billion, but the pace accelerated dramatically — each subsequent billion took only 33, 14, 13, 12, 12, and 11 years respectively.

| 2 billion  | 1927        | 123                          |
| 3 billion  | 1960        | 33                           |
| 4 billion  | 1974        | 14                           |
| 5 billion  | 1987        | 13                           |
| 6 billion  | 1999        | 12                           |
| 7 billion  | 2011        | 12                           |
| 8 billion  | 2022        | 11                           |

(Source: Worldometers estimates, via Wikipedia's "World population" article)

A few notable observations:
- It took all of human history until **1804** to reach 1 billion people.
- The next billion took **123 years** (to 1927).
- Each subsequent billion came faster, with the gap dropping to as little as **11 years** between 7 and 8 billion.

command_log: agent-browser open "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population" && agent-browser wait 2000 && agent-browser get text "table.wikitable tbody tr:nth-child(-n+5)"

agent-browser eval "const rows = document.querySelectorAll('table.wikitable tbody tr'); const results = []; for(let i=0; i<6; i++){ const cells = rows[i]?.querySelectorAll('td,th'); if(cells) results.push(Array.from(cells).map(c=>c.innerText.trim()).join(' | ')); } return results.join('\n');"

agent-browser eval "(function(){ const rows = document.querySelectorAll('table.wikitable tbody tr'); const results = []; for(let i=0; i<6; i++){ const cells = rows[i]?.querySelectorAll('td,th'); if(cells) results.push(Array.from(cells).map(c=>c.innerText.trim()).join(' | ')); } return results.join('\n'); })()"

grade.details: The agent successfully navigated to the correct Wikipedia URL, extracted the table data using JavaScript evaluation, and correctly reported the top 3 countries as India (1,417,492,000), China (1,404,890,000), and United States (341,784,857), which matches the known facts.

The [rust-lang/rust](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust) repository has **112k stars**.

**3. rust-lang.org — Tagline / Main Description**
The site's "Why Rust?" section highlights three pillars:
- **Performance** — "Rust is blazingly fast and memory-efficient: with no runtime or garbage collector..."
- **Reliability** — "Rust's rich type system and ownership model guarantee memory-safety and thread-safety..."
- **Productivity** — "Rust has great documentation, a friendly compiler with useful error messages, and top-notch tooling..."
